skill.name: design-component
skill.description: Guidance for designing culturally sensitive storyteller cards, story cards, profile displays, AI enrichment patterns, and consent dashboard components.
skill.summary: Guides culturally sensitive storyteller cards, profile displays, AI enrichment, and consent UI components.
skill.author: Acurioustractor

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are Markdown code fences, inline file references, and substring matches inside ordinary documentation. The only retained risk factor is a relative fetch example for an application API endpoint; no external domain, credential handling, malware intent, or prompt injection evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as External Commands
low_findings.description: TRUE EVALUATION: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged backticks are Markdown fences and inline code examples in documentation, not executable Ruby or shell backtick calls. The reviewed files describe TypeScript, TSX, SQL, and design guidance, with no evidence of command execution intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are visibly Markdown code blocks or inline code references. No shell invocation, Ruby backtick execution, or user-controlled command path was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Documentation Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: TRUE EVALUATION: FALSE POSITIVE. The high-severity weak cryptography hits do not show MD5, SHA1, DES, or cryptographic API use. Examples include normal words such as design, description, manual_overrides, and sacred content guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct review of the flagged lines found ordinary prose, Markdown headings, SQL examples, and UI guidance. There is no cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm selection at these locations.

low_findings.title: Relative Fetch Example Requires Normal Application Review
low_findings.description: TRUE EVALUATION: LOW-RISK TRUE POSITIVE. The skill includes a fetch call to a relative application endpoint for storyteller enrichment. It does not contact an external domain or transmit secrets, but implementations should still validate consent, authentication, and server-side authorization.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network call is clearly present and uses a relative URL under the same application. The surrounding code sends enrichment options only, so the security concern is implementation hygiene rather than exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent UI patterns for storyteller profiles, story cards, and consent workflows. This skill provides data models, component variants, cultural safeguards, and AI enrichment guidance for respectful product design.
actual_capabilities: Defines storyteller card data fields, priority tiers, and optional AI-enriched fields.
Documents default, compact, featured, list, mini, and profile header card patterns.
Provides UI examples for badges, quotes, cultural background, metrics, and loading states.
Describes AI enrichment flows for bios, quotes, themes, summaries, and connections.
Recommends consent dashboard patterns, token masking, revocation dialogs, and cultural permission indicators.
Summarizes card consolidation, dark mode, filtering, and theme taxonomy recommendations.
limitations: This is documentation guidance, not a packaged component library.
API endpoints and component paths are examples that must be adapted to the target codebase.
Cultural decisions still require review by appropriate community stakeholders.
Security controls for real tokens, consent, and access must be implemented separately.

use_cases.title: Create storyteller profile cards
use_cases.description: Use the field hierarchy and variant guidance to design cards that show identity, culture, story metrics, and optional AI summaries.

use_cases.title: Plan AI enrichment displays
use_cases.description: Map enrichment outputs to badges, quotes, theme indicators, profile summaries, and user override states.

use_cases.title: Review consent dashboard interactions
use_cases.description: Apply the consent badge, token masking, revocation dialog, and cultural permission patterns to syndication workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a card variant
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: select a layout for a simple profile list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design-component skill to recommend the best storyteller card variant for a directory page. Include visible fields, fallback states, and accessibility notes.

prompt_templates.title: Map profile data to UI
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: convert available data into card content.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design-component skill to map these storyteller profile fields into a respectful card layout. Separate always visible fields from hover or profile-only fields.

prompt_templates.title: Design AI enrichment states
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: add AI-generated content with user control.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design-component skill to design AI enrichment states for bio summaries, featured quotes, theme expertise, and manual overrides. Include loading, complete, and error states.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a consent UI flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: review syndication and revocation interactions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the design-component skill to audit a consent management dashboard for cultural permission levels, token masking, revocation messaging, analytics display, and unsafe interaction patterns.

output_examples.input: A designer asks which fields belong on a featured storyteller card.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend identity, cultural background, story count, elder or featured badges, a selected quote, top themes, languages, and clear profile actions.

output_examples.input: An engineer asks how to show AI-enhanced profile content safely.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest visible AI indicators, manual override notices, consent-aware quote handling, and rules that prevent automatic publication of sensitive content.

output_examples.input: A product manager asks how to design revocation controls.
output_examples.output: The skill would outline status badges, clear consequence messaging, reversible consent language, optional reason capture, and controls that avoid pressure or fear.

best_practices: Show cultural background, traditional territory, and elder status with respectful labels and clear hierarchy.
Mark AI-generated content clearly and preserve storyteller control over suggested text or quotes.
Use semantic colors, accessible contrast, keyboard navigation, and visible focus states across card variants.
anti_patterns: Do not auto-publish AI summaries, quotes, or tags without user or community review.
Do not extract or promote sacred or restricted story content in public cards.
Do not rely on hardcoded colors, duplicate card variants, or inconsistent filter naming.

faq.question: Does this skill generate production React components?
faq.answer: No. It provides design and implementation guidance that engineers can adapt into production components.

faq.question: Can it help with cultural sensitivity requirements?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes display rules, permission indicators, and AI safeguards, but human cultural review is still required.

faq.question: What card types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers storyteller cards, story cards, profile headers, list rows, compact cards, featured cards, and consent dashboard cards.

faq.question: Does it include AI enrichment guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes bio enhancement, quote extraction, theme analysis, connection suggestions, and summary generation patterns.

faq.question: Is the network example a security issue?
faq.answer: The fetch example uses a relative application endpoint. Implementations still need authentication, authorization, consent checks, and input validation.

faq.question: Can it be used outside Empathy Ledger?
faq.answer: Yes. The patterns are adaptable, but names, endpoints, data models, and cultural governance must match the target project.
